# Exodus Chapter 26 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Leningrad Codex (B19A), Folios ~47r–48r (PDF pp. 92–94); Codex Vaticanus (Vat.gr.1209), pp. 68–69 (LXX) | **Source Image:** `source-texts/hebrew-OT/EXO_026_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_026_page_002.jpg`, `EXO_026_page_003.jpg`; `source-texts/greek-OT-LXX/EXO_026_vaticanus_page_001.jpg`, `EXO_026_vaticanus_page_002.jpg`

---

## The Curtains of the Tabernacle (vv. 1–6)

**1** "And the Tabernacle [**et-hammishkan**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] with ten [**eser**] curtains [**yeri'ot**] of fine twisted linen [**shesh moshzar**] and blue [**utekheleth**] and purple [**ve'argaman**] and scarlet yarn [**vetola'at shani**]; with cherubim [**keruvim**], the work [**ma'aseh**] of a skilled craftsman [**choshev**], you shall make [**ta'aseh**] them [**otam**].

**2** The length [**orekh**] of each [**ha'achat**] curtain [**hayeri'ah**] shall be twenty-eight [**shemoneh ve'esrim**] cubits [**ba'ammah**], and the width [**verochav**] of each [**ha'achat**] curtain [**hayeri'ah**] four [**arba**] cubits [**ba'ammah**]; one [**middah**] measure [**achat**] for all [**lekhol**] the curtains [**hayeri'ot**].

**3** Five [**chamesh**] of the curtains [**hayeri'ot**] shall be joined [**choverot**] each [**ishshah**] to the other [**el-achotah**], and five [**vechamesh**] curtains [**yeri'ot**] joined [**choverot**] each [**ishshah**] to the other [**el-achotah**].

**4** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] loops [**lula'ot**] of blue [**tekheleth**] on [**al**] the edge [**sefat**] of the one [**ha'achat**] curtain [**hayeri'ah**] at the end [**miqqatseh**] of the first coupling [**bachoveret**], and likewise [**vekhen**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] on [**al**] the edge [**sefat**] of the curtain [**hayeri'ah**] that is outermost [**haqqitsonah**] in the second [**hasshenit**] coupling [**bamachberet**].

**5** Fifty [**chamishim**] loops [**lula'ot**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] on the one [**ha'achat**] curtain [**bayeri'ah**], and fifty [**vachamishim**] loops [**lula'ot**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] on the end [**biqtseh**] of the curtain [**hayeri'ah**] that [**asher**] is in the second [**hasshenit**] coupling [**bamachberet**]; the loops [**hallula'ot**] shall be opposite [**maqbilot**] one to another [**ishshah el-achotah**].

**6** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] fifty [**chamishim**] clasps [**qarsey**] of gold [**zahav**], and you shall join [**vechibbarta**] the curtains [**et-hayeri'ot**] each to the other [**ishshah el-achotah**] with the clasps [**baqqerasim**], and the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**] shall be [**vehayah**] one [**echad**].

---

## The Curtains of Goats' Hair (vv. 7–13)

**7** "And you shall make [**ve'asita**] curtains [**yeri'ot**] of goats' hair [**izzim**] for a tent [**le'ohel**] over [**al**] the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**]; eleven [**ashtey-esreh**] curtains [**yeri'ot**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] them [**otam**].

**8** The length [**orekh**] of each [**ha'achat**] curtain [**hayeri'ah**] shall be thirty [**sheloshim**] cubits [**ba'ammah**], and the width [**verechev**] of each [**ha'achat**] curtain [**hayeri'ah**] four [**arba**] cubits [**ba'ammah**]; one [**middah**] measure [**achat**] for the eleven [**le'ashtey esreh**] curtains [**yeri'ot**].

**9** And you shall join [**vechibbarta**] five [**chamesh**] of the curtains [**hayeri'ot**] by themselves [**levad**] and six [**ve'et-shesh**] of the curtains [**hayeri'ot**] by themselves [**levad**], and you shall double over [**vekhafalta**] the sixth [**et-hayeri'ah**] curtain [**hashshishit**] at the front [**el-mul**] of the face [**peney**] of the tent [**ha'ohel**].

**10** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] fifty [**chamishim**] loops [**lula'ot**] on the edge [**al sefat**] of the one [**ha'achat**] curtain [**hayeri'ah**] that is outermost [**haqqitsonah**] in the first coupling [**bachoveret**], and fifty [**vachamishim**] loops [**lula'ot**] on the edge [**al sefat**] of the curtain [**hayeri'ah**] of the second [**hasshenit**] coupling [**hamachberet**].

**11** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] fifty [**chamishim**] clasps [**qarsey**] of bronze [**nechoshet**] and put [**veheve'ta**] the clasps [**et-haqqerasim**] into the loops [**ballula'ot**] and join [**vechibbarta**] the tent [**et-ha'ohel**] so that it shall be [**vehayah**] one [**echad**].

**12** And the overhanging [**vehassoreach**] excess [**ha'odef**] in the curtains [**biyri'ot**] of the tent [**ha'ohel**] — the half [**chatsi**] curtain [**hayeri'ah**] that remains over [**ha'odefet**] — shall hang over [**tisrach**] the back [**al achorey**] of the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**].

**13** And the cubit [**veha'ammah**] on this side [**mizzeh**] and the cubit [**veha'ammah**] on that side [**mizzeh**] of the excess [**ba'odef**] in the length [**be'orekh**] of the curtains [**yeri'ot**] of the tent [**ha'ohel**] shall hang over [**yihyeh**] the sides [**al-tsiddey**] of the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**], on this side [**mizzeh**] and on that side [**umizzeh**], to cover it [**lekhassoto**].

---

## The Coverings of the Tabernacle (vv. 14)

**14** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] a covering [**mikhseh**] for the tent [**la'ohel**] of rams' [**orot eylim**] skins [**orot**] dyed red [**me'oddamim**], and a covering [**umikh'seh**] of tahash [**orot techashim**] skins above [**milma'lah**].

---

## The Frames of the Tabernacle (vv. 15–30)

**15** "And you shall make [**ve'asita**] the frames [**et-haqqerashim**] for the Tabernacle [**lammishkan**] of acacia [**atsey**] wood [**shittim**], standing upright [**omedim**].

**16** Ten [**eser**] cubits [**ammot**] shall be the length [**orekh**] of each frame [**haqqaresh**] and a cubit [**ve'ammah**] and a half [**vachetsi**] cubit [**ha'ammah**] the width [**rochav**] of each [**ha'echad**] frame [**haqqaresh**].

**17** Two [**shtey**] tenons [**yadot**] for each [**la**] frame [**qqaresh ha'echad**], joined [**meshullvot**] one [**ishshah**] to the other [**el-achotah**]; so [**ken**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] for all [**lekhol**] the frames [**qarshey**] of the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**].

**18** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] the frames [**et-haqqerashim**] for the Tabernacle [**lammishkan**]: twenty [**esrim**] frames [**qaresh**] for the south [**negbah**] side [**teymanah**] southward [**teymanah**].

**19** And forty [**ve'arba'im**] bases [**adney-khesef**] of silver [**khesef**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] under [**tachat**] the twenty [**esrim**] frames [**haqqaresh**]: two [**shney**] bases [**adanim**] under [**tachat**] the one [**haqqeresh ha'echad**] frame [**haqqeresh**] for its two [**lishtey**] tenons [**yadotav**], and two [**ushney**] bases [**adanim**] under [**tachat**] the other [**haqqeresh ha'echad**] frame [**haqqeresh**] for its two [**lishtey**] tenons [**yadotav**].

**20** And for the second [**hassheni**] side [**uletse'la**] of the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**], on the north [**tsafon**] side [**lif'at**], twenty [**esrim**] frames [**qaresh**],

**21** and their forty [**ve'arba'im**] bases [**adneyhem**] of silver [**khesef**]: two [**shney**] bases [**adanim**] under [**tachat**] the one [**haqqeresh ha'echad**] frame [**haqqeresh**], and two [**ushney**] bases [**adanim**] under [**tachat**] the other [**haqqeresh ha'echad**] frame [**haqqeresh**].

**22** And for the rear [**uleyarketey**] of the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**] westward [**yammah**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] six [**shishah**] frames [**qerashim**].

**23** And two [**ushney**] frames [**qerashim**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] for the corners [**limqutso'ot**] of the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**] at the rear [**bayyarketayim**].

**24** And they shall be [**veyihyu**] doubled [**to'amim**] from below [**millera'**], and together [**yachdav**] they shall be [**yihyu**] complete [**tammim**] at [**al**] its top [**ro'sho**], unto [**el**] the one [**hattabba'at ha'achat**] ring [**tabba'at**]; thus [**ken**] it shall be [**yihyeh**] for both of them [**lishneyhem**]; for the two [**lishtey**] corners [**hammiqtso'ot**] they shall be [**yihyu**].

**25** And there shall be [**vehayu**] eight [**shemonah**] frames [**qerashim**] and their bases [**ve'adneyhem**] of silver [**khesef**], sixteen [**shishah asar**] bases [**adanim**]: two [**shney**] bases [**adanim**] under [**tachat**] the one [**haqqeresh ha'echad**] frame [**haqqeresh**] and two [**ushney**] bases [**adanim**] under [**tachat**] the other [**haqqeresh ha'echad**] frame [**haqqeresh**].

**26** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] bars [**verichey**] of acacia [**atsey**] wood [**shittim**]: five [**chamishah**] for the frames [**leqarshey**] of the one [**ha'echad**] side [**tsela**] of the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**],

**27** and five [**vachamishah**] bars [**verichim**] for the frames [**leqarshey**] of the second [**hassheni**] side [**tsela**] of the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**], and five [**vachamishah**] bars [**verichim**] for the frames [**leqarshey**] of the side [**tsela**] of the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**] at the rear [**layyarketayim**] westward [**yammah**].

**28** And the middle [**hattikon**] bar [**habberiyach**] in the midst [**betokh**] of the frames [**haqqerashim**] shall run [**movriach**] from end [**min-haqqatseh**] to end [**el-haqqatseh**].

**29** And the frames [**ve'et-haqqerashim**] you shall overlay [**tetsappeh**] with gold [**zahav**], and their rings [**ve'et-tabb'oteyhem**] you shall make [**ta'aseh**] of gold [**zahav**] as holders [**battim**] for the bars [**labberichim**], and you shall overlay [**vetsippita**] the bars [**et-habberichim**] with gold [**zahav**].

**30** And you shall erect [**vahaqemota**] the Tabernacle [**et-hammishkan**] according to its plan [**kemishpato**] which [**asher**] you were shown [**her'eyta**] on the mountain [**bahar**].

---

## The Veil (vv. 31–35)

**31** "And you shall make [**ve'asita**] a veil [**farokhet**] of blue [**tekheleth**] and purple [**ve'argaman**] and scarlet yarn [**vetola'at shani**] and fine twisted linen [**veshesh moshzar**]; the work [**ma'aseh**] of a skilled craftsman [**choshev**] he shall make [**ya'aseh**] it [**otah**], with cherubim [**keruvim**].

**32** And you shall hang [**venatattha**] it [**otah**] on [**al**] four [**arba'ah**] pillars [**ammudey**] of acacia [**shittim**] overlaid [**metsuppim**] with gold [**zahav**]; their hooks [**vaveyhem**] shall be of gold [**zahav**], upon [**al**] four [**arba'ah**] bases [**adney**] of silver [**khesef**].

**33** And you shall hang [**venatattha**] the veil [**et-happarokhet**] under [**tachat**] the clasps [**haqqerasim**], and you shall bring [**veheve'ta**] there [**shammah**], within [**mibbeyt**] the veil [**lapparokhet**], the ark [**et aron**] of the testimony [**ha'edut**]; and the veil [**happarokhet**] shall separate [**vehivdilah**] for you [**lakhem**] between [**beyn**] the Holy Place [**haqqodesh**] and [**uveyn**] the Most Holy Place [**qodesh haqqodashim**].

**34** And you shall put [**venatattha**] the mercy seat [**et-hakkapporet**] upon [**al**] the ark [**aron**] of the testimony [**ha'edut**] in the Most Holy Place [**beqodesh haqqodashim**].

**35** And you shall set [**vesamta**] the table [**et-hashulchan**] outside [**michutz**] the veil [**lapparokhet**], and the lampstand [**ve'et-hammenorah**] opposite [**nokhach**] the table [**hashulchan**] on [**al**] the south side [**tsela**] of the Tabernacle [**hammishkan**] southward [**teymanah**]; and the table [**vehashulchan**] you shall put [**titten**] on [**al**] the north [**tsela**] side [**tsafon**].

---

## The Screen for the Entrance (vv. 36–37)

**36** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] a screen [**masakh**] for the entrance [**lefetach**] of the tent [**ha'ohel**], of blue [**tekheleth**] and purple [**ve'argaman**] and scarlet yarn [**vetola'at shani**] and fine twisted linen [**veshesh moshzar**], the work [**ma'aseh**] of an embroiderer [**roqem**].

**37** And you shall make [**ve'asita**] for the screen [**lammasakh**] five [**chamishah**] pillars [**ammudey**] of acacia [**shittim**] and overlay [**vetsippita**] them [**otam**] with gold [**zahav**], and their hooks [**vaveyhem**] shall be of gold [**zahav**]; and you shall cast [**veyatsaqta**] for them [**lahem**] five [**chamishah**] bases [**adney**] of bronze [**nechoshet**].

---

## Translator's Notes

### Key Terms

**yeri'ah / yeri'ot** (vv. 1–13) -- "curtain / curtains." The noun yeri'ah (plural yeri'ot) denotes a large woven cloth panel or drape. The root is y-r-' ("to tremble, shake"), likely referring to the way fabric quivers in the wind. The Tabernacle's innermost layer consists of ten such curtains of fine linen woven with blue, purple, and scarlet yarn and decorated with cherubim designs. These inner curtains constitute the mishkan proper -- the beautiful fabric ceiling that would be visible from inside the sanctuary. Over these are placed the coarser goats' hair curtains (v. 7), which form the ohel ("tent") as a protective outer layer. The distinction between mishkan (the inner curtain structure) and ohel (the outer goats' hair tent) is maintained consistently in the Hebrew text, though in later usage the terms become interchangeable. The LXX renders yeri'ot as aulaia ("curtains, hangings"), the term from which English "awning" may ultimately derive.

**shesh moshzar** (vv. 1, 31, 36) -- "fine twisted linen." The noun shesh denotes fine Egyptian linen (the Hebrew word is likely a loanword from Egyptian), and the passive participle moshzar (from sh-z-r, "to twist, twine") describes the twisting of multiple threads together into a strong, tightly woven fabric. The combination denotes the highest quality textile of the ancient world -- Egyptian linen was renowned for its fineness and whiteness. The threefold coloring of blue (tekheleth), purple (argaman), and scarlet (tola'at shani) dyed into this white linen base creates the distinctive Tabernacle palette. Tekheleth is a blue-violet dye extracted from the murex snail; argaman is a reddish-purple from the same source but at a different stage of processing; tola'at shani ("worm of scarlet") is a crimson dye derived from the kermes insect. These were the costliest dyes of antiquity. The LXX renders shesh moshzar as busson keklosmenon ("twisted byssus/fine linen").

**choshev** (vv. 1, 31) -- "skilled craftsman" or "designer." The active participle of ch-sh-v ("to think, devise, plan") denotes one who designs and executes complex woven patterns -- literally a "thinker" or "deviser." The ma'aseh choshev ("work of a skilled designer") refers to tapestry weaving in which the design (here, cherubim) is woven into the fabric during the manufacturing process, as opposed to embroidery (ma'aseh roqem, v. 36) where the design is stitched onto the finished cloth afterward. The choshev produces a more integrated, higher-quality work: the cherubim on the inner curtains and the veil are part of the fabric itself. The LXX renders ma'aseh choshev as ergon huphantos ("work of a weaver"), and the Vaticanus text consistently uses this phrase for the skilled tapestry work.

**qeresh / qerashim** (vv. 15–29) -- "frame / frames." The noun qeresh (plural qerashim) is traditionally translated "board" or "plank," but its precise meaning has been debated. A solid plank ten cubits high and one and a half cubits wide would be enormously heavy and require massive amounts of acacia wood, which grows only as a modest desert tree. Many scholars now understand qeresh as an open frame or lattice-work panel, supported by the tenons (yadot) at its base that fit into silver bases (adanim). The qerashim form the structural skeleton of the Tabernacle -- twenty frames on the south, twenty on the north, six on the west (rear), and two corner frames -- over which the curtain layers are draped. The LXX renders qeresh as stulos ("pillar, upright post") in some verses, suggesting that the ancient Greek translators also understood something more like vertical supports than solid planks.

**adanim** (vv. 19–25) -- "bases, sockets." The noun eden (plural adanim) denotes the heavy silver pedestals into which the tenons (yadot) of the frames are inserted. Each frame has two tenons fitting into two bases, and there are forty bases for the south wall, forty for the north, and sixteen for the west -- a total of ninety-six silver bases for the frames alone, plus four more for the veil pillars (v. 32). According to Exodus 38:27, each base weighed one talent (kikkar) of silver, approximately 75 pounds / 34 kg. The total weight of silver for the bases alone was thus approximately 7,200 pounds / 3,265 kg -- a staggering amount that grounded the Tabernacle both literally and symbolically. The word eden may be related to Akkadian adannu ("fixed base, foundation"), and the LXX renders it as basis ("base, pedestal").

**parokhet** (vv. 31–35) -- "veil, curtain." The noun parokhet (from p-r-k, "to separate, divide") denotes the heavy woven curtain that separates the Holy Place (qodesh) from the Most Holy Place (qodesh haqqodashim). Like the inner curtains, it is made of blue, purple, scarlet yarn, and fine twisted linen, with cherubim woven into it by the skilled craftsman (choshev). The veil is the definitive boundary of sacred space -- it marks the threshold between the accessible and the inaccessible, between the area where priests minister daily and the innermost chamber where only the high priest may enter, and only once a year on Yom Kippur (Lev 16). The tearing of this veil at Christ's death (Matt 27:51; Mark 15:38; Luke 23:45) is one of the most dramatic moments in the New Testament, signifying that the barrier between God and humanity has been removed. The LXX renders parokhet as katapetasma ("veil, curtain that hangs down"), the same term used in the Synoptic Gospel accounts of the temple veil's rending. Hebrews 10:20 describes the veil as representing Christ's flesh -- "through the veil, that is, His flesh" -- making the parokhet a type of the incarnation through which access to God is granted.

**masakh** (vv. 36–37) -- "screen, covering." The noun masakh (from s-k-k, "to cover, screen") denotes the screen or curtain that hangs at the entrance to the tent. Unlike the parokhet (which is woven with cherubim by a choshev, a skilled designer), the masakh is the work of a roqem ("embroiderer") -- a less exalted craft. This distinction in workmanship corresponds to a distinction in sanctity: the parokhet guards the Most Holy Place, while the masakh guards the entrance to the Holy Place. The masakh is also simpler in its support structure -- five pillars of acacia wood with gold overlay, set in bronze bases (not silver, as for the veil's pillars). The graduated materials (gold bases for the Ark, silver for the veil pillars, bronze for the entrance screen) reflect the decreasing gradient of holiness from the center outward.

### Grammatical Notes

**ta'aseh otam keruvim** (v. 1) -- "you shall make them with cherubim." The noun keruvim stands in apposition or as an adverbial accusative specifying the decorative design to be woven into the curtains. The phrase ma'aseh choshev ("work of a skilled craftsman") is a construct chain functioning adverbially to describe the technique of production. The entire clause instructs that the cherubim design is to be integrated into the fabric by the skilled weaver, not applied afterward. The cherubim on the inner curtains would be the only decorative element visible to the priests serving inside the sanctuary -- looking up, they would see winged figures woven into the fabric overhead, a visual echo of the cherubim atop the Ark behind the veil.

**choverot ishshah el-achotah** (v. 3) -- "joined, each to her sister." The feminine reciprocal phrase ishshah el-achotah (literally "a woman to her sister") is the standard Hebrew idiom for things joined in matching pairs, using the feminine form because yeri'ah ("curtain") is a feminine noun. The participle choverot (Qal feminine plural of ch-v-r, "to join, bind") indicates the panels are sewn or connected together edge to edge. The five-plus-five arrangement creates two large composite curtains that are then joined to each other by the golden clasps (v. 6), forming a single unified covering.

**vehivdilah lakhem beyn haqqodesh uveyn qodesh haqqodashim** (v. 33) -- "and it shall separate for you between the Holy Place and the Most Holy Place." The Hiphil perfect of b-d-l ("to separate, divide") with vav-consecutive expresses the veil's function: it makes a division. The root b-d-l is the same verb used in Genesis 1 for God's acts of separation (light from darkness, waters above from waters below, land from sea). The creation-Tabernacle parallel is not accidental: the Tabernacle is a microcosm, and its internal divisions mirror the divisions of creation. The phrase qodesh haqqodashim ("holy of holies" or "most holy place") is a Hebrew superlative formed by the construct of a noun with its own plural -- the "holiness of holinesses," that is, the most intensely holy space. This same grammatical construction appears in shir hashshirim ("song of songs," i.e., the greatest song) and melekh hammelakhim ("king of kings"). The LXX renders this as to hagion kai to hagion tōn hagiōn ("the holy place and the holy of holies").

**kemishpato asher her'eyta bahar** (v. 30) -- "according to its plan which you were shown on the mountain." The noun mishpat here means "plan, proper procedure, due order" (from sh-p-t, "to judge, govern") -- not its more common meaning of "justice/judgment" but its secondary sense of "prescribed manner, ordinance." The verb her'eyta is Hophal perfect second masculine singular of r-'a-h ("to see, show"): "you were caused to see, you were shown." This verse echoes the framing command of 25:9 and 25:40 -- the Tabernacle must be built according to the heavenly pattern revealed on Sinai. The repetition of this principle at the transition between the curtain/covering instructions and the frame instructions reinforces that every detail of the construction derives from the divine blueprint, not human improvisation. The LXX renders kemishpato as kata to eidos ("according to the form/appearance"), and the Vaticanus text preserves this reading clearly.

### Structural Notes

Exodus 26 forms the architectural heart of the Tabernacle instruction. While chapter 25 described the furniture (Ark, Table, Lampstand), chapter 26 describes the structure itself -- the building that houses the furniture. The chapter moves logically from inside to outside and from top to bottom: (1) the inner linen curtains with their cherubim (vv. 1-6), (2) the outer goats' hair curtains (vv. 7-13), (3) the protective skin coverings (v. 14), (4) the structural frames (vv. 15-30), (5) the veil separating the two chambers (vv. 31-35), and (6) the entrance screen (vv. 36-37). This ordering is architecturally reverse from what a builder would construct -- one would erect the frames first, then drape the curtains -- but it follows the same divine-perspective logic as chapter 25: God describes the Tabernacle beginning with what is nearest to His presence (the cherubim-adorned inner curtains visible from the divine throne) and moving outward.

The chapter's internal symmetry is notable. The inner curtains are joined by fifty gold clasps (v. 6); the outer curtains are joined by fifty bronze clasps (v. 11). The inner curtains number ten (arranged as five plus five); the outer curtains number eleven (arranged as five plus six). The frame section is organized by compass direction: south (vv. 18-19), north (vv. 20-21), west/rear (vv. 22-25), with the bar system unifying all three walls (vv. 26-28). The veil section (vv. 31-35) establishes the crucial internal division, while the screen section (vv. 36-37) establishes the boundary between inside and outside.

The graduated materials reflect a theology of holiness-as-proximity. Gold clasps join the inner curtains; bronze clasps join the outer. The veil pillars stand in silver bases; the screen pillars stand in bronze bases. The inner curtains bear cherubim woven by a choshev (skilled designer); the entrance screen is the work of a roqem (embroiderer) without cherubim. The closer to God's presence, the more precious the materials and the more exalted the craftsmanship.

### Theological Notes

The Tabernacle's layered construction -- fine linen, then goats' hair, then rams' skins dyed red, then tahash skins -- creates a structure that is glorious within and plain without. A worshiper approaching from outside would see only the rough outer covering of animal skins; only those who entered would see the splendor of the cherubim-adorned linen panels. This architectural principle embodies a theology of hidden glory: God's most magnificent self-revelation is reserved for those who draw near. The external plainness and internal beauty of the Tabernacle anticipates the prophetic description of the Servant of YHWH: "He had no form or majesty that we should look at Him, and no beauty that we should desire Him" (Isa 53:2) -- outward humility concealing inward glory.

The veil (parokhet) is the most theologically significant element in this chapter. It performs the priestly function of separation (havdalah) between the holy and the most holy, using the same root (b-d-l) employed in Genesis 1 for the primordial separations of creation. The veil declares that holiness requires boundaries: not everyone may approach God's immediate presence, not at any time, not in any manner. The veil is simultaneously a barrier and a threshold -- it prevents unauthorized access while implying that authorized access is possible. The high priest passes through it once a year (Lev 16), bearing blood. The New Testament's identification of the veil with Christ's flesh (Heb 10:20) and the rending of the veil at the crucifixion (Matt 27:51) transform the barrier into a gateway: what once separated now provides "a new and living way" of access to God.

The spatial arrangement prescribed in verses 33-35 establishes the fundamental sacred geography of Israelite worship. The Ark with its mercy seat resides in the western chamber (the Most Holy Place), separated by the veil. The Table stands on the north side and the Lampstand on the south side of the eastern chamber (the Holy Place). This arrangement maps onto cosmic symbolism: the Lampstand with its seven lights on the south may represent the heavenly luminaries; the Table with its twelve loaves on the north may represent God's provision for the twelve tribes; the Ark in the west -- the direction of the setting sun and the abode of God -- represents the divine throne. The Tabernacle is thus a microcosm: heaven and earth in miniature, with the veil as the boundary between the heavenly (Most Holy Place) and the terrestrial (Holy Place) realms.

The command to erect the Tabernacle "according to its plan which you were shown on the mountain" (v. 30) reiterates the foundational principle established in 25:9, 40: the earthly sanctuary is a replica of a heavenly original. Moses is not improvising; he is copying. This principle of heavenly archetype and earthly copy will be developed in Second Temple Judaism (the heavenly temple of 1 Enoch and the Songs of the Sabbath Sacrifice from Qumran) and reaches its fullest theological articulation in Hebrews 8:1-5, where the author declares that the earthly priests "serve a copy and shadow of the heavenly things" and quotes the command to Moses: "See that you make everything according to the pattern shown you on the mountain." The Tabernacle's derivative character -- its status as copy, not original -- both dignifies it (it mirrors heaven) and relativizes it (it is not heaven itself).

---

*Note on the LXX (Vaticanus):* The Vaticanus text of Exodus 26 (pages 68-69) preserves the LXX version with close fidelity to the MT's structural description, though with several notable renderings. At v. 1, the LXX renders yeri'ot as aulaia ("curtains") and ma'aseh choshev as ergon huphantos ("work of a weaver"), interpreting the skilled designer as specifically a weaver. At v. 1, shesh moshzar is rendered bussou keklosmenēs ("of twisted byssus"), using the Greek term byssos for fine Egyptian linen. At v. 7, the goats' hair curtains for the ohel are described as derris ("skin covering, leather curtain") in some LXX manuscripts, though Vaticanus preserves the distinction between the inner aulaia and the outer tent covering. At v. 14, the problematic orot techashim ("tahash skins") is rendered dermata huakinthina ("blue/hyacinth-colored skins"), interpreting the unknown animal techash as a color designation -- a significant interpretive move that influenced later tradition. At v. 15, qerashim is rendered stuloi ("pillars, posts"), suggesting the translators understood the structural elements as upright posts rather than broad planks. At v. 24, the difficult verse describing the corner frames is rendered with some expansion for clarity. At v. 30, kemishpato is rendered kata to eidos to dedeigmenon soi en tō orei ("according to the form shown to you on the mountain"), reinforcing the pattern-language of 25:9, 40. At v. 31, the LXX renders parokhet as katapetasma ("veil"), the term that becomes standard in the New Testament for the temple veil whose rending signals the opening of access to God. At v. 33, the crucial theological statement about the veil separating the Holy Place from the Most Holy Place is rendered faithfully: dioristei to katapetasma humin ana meson tou hagiou kai ana meson tou hagiou tōn hagiōn ("the veil shall divide for you between the holy place and between the holy of holies"). At v. 36, the distinction between choshev-work and roqem-work is preserved: the entrance screen is poikiltou ("of variegated/embroidered work") rather than huphantos ("of woven work"). The Vaticanus manuscript shows the text of Exodus 26 continuing from the lower portion of page 68 through the upper and middle portions of page 69, with clear section markers and consistent uncial script across the three-column layout.

--- LOGOS
